text: The surgeon uses two O-vicryl sutures on a CT-one needle, each cut to six inches, to close the renal capsule. Each suture is secured with a knot and a hemlock clip.
The surgeon passes the suture through the renal capsule, pulling it through until the hemlock clip is visibly anchored on the capsule surface.
The surgeon passes the suture through the renal capsule on the opposite side of the defect, while the assistant applies a large hemlock clip to secure the suture in the middle.

The console surgeon uses the sliding hemlock clip technique to slide the clip down the suture, approximating the renal capsular edges to close the defect. Additional interrupted sutures are placed across the defect to aid in closure and distribute tension, with the option for a running closure based on surgeon preference.

The surgeon holds the suture perpendicular to the renal capsule while sliding down the hemlock clip, preventing the suture from tearing through the renal parenchyma. The upper pole defect is closed using the same technique.
The surgeon places the most challenging stitches first, using a left-handed and back-handed technique to position the suture for easier clip placement by the assistant.
The surgeon uses previously placed capsular sutures to retract the kidney, enhancing exposure for additional needle throws during the nephrectomy procedure.
